common_name: Black-Eyed Susan Vine (Thunbergia Annual Tropical Climbing Vine)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; soak seeds overnight in warm water before starting to improve germination. In zone 9, transplant March–April and plant in a location with trellis or support to climb. Black-Eyed Susan Vine is a perennial in zone 10–11 (frost-free) but grown as an annual in zone 9 where it blooms prolifically from late spring through fall. Excellent in hanging baskets where the vining branches cascade attractively, and on small trellises and fence posts.

directions: Start seeds indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost at 65–70°F; transplant to a trellis or support after last frost. Black-Eyed Susan Vine (not related to the native perennial Rudbeckia) is a fast-growing African tropical vine with a twining habit that quickly covers a small trellis, fence section, or mailbox post with dense foliage and continuous bloom. The distinctive 1–1.5 inch flowers have five flattened petals in a circular arrangement around a dark purple-black or white center tube — the dark center among the orange or yellow petals creating the characteristic 'black-eyed' appearance that gives the vine its common name. Colors range across orange, yellow, white, and salmon, with the classic orange-with-black-center the most recognizable form. Black-Eyed Susan Vine is particularly valuable in the Gulf South for its heat and humidity tolerance — it blooms continuously through hot summers when many other annual vines fade or slow down. The vine is well-scaled for residential use: reaching 5–8 feet rather than the 20–30 feet of aggressive vines like trumpet creeper, making it appropriate for small gardens, fences, and containers with a tomato cage for support.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Vines for Florida Gardens
Missouri Botanical Garden — Thunbergia alata plant profile
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Ornamental annual vine descriptions

common_name: Heavenly Blue Morning Glory (Classic Large-Flowered Blue Annual Vine)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost (morning glories resent transplanting); nick or soak seeds overnight before planting to soften the hard seed coat and dramatically improve germination. In zone 9, plant March–April on a fence or trellis in full sun — avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen, which produces lush vines but fewer flowers. Morning glories are short-day plants and bloom more prolifically as days shorten in late summer.

directions: Nick seed coat with a file or soak overnight, then direct sow 0.5 inch deep after last frost; thin to 6–12 inches along a trellis or fence. Heavenly Blue is the most widely recognized and grown morning glory variety in the United States — an heirloom Ipomoea tricolor type producing large (3–4 inch diameter) flowers of an extraordinary clear sky-blue with a white star pattern at the center and white throat, fading to pinkish-lavender as the day progresses. The color is remarkable for its purity and intensity — true blue flowers are uncommon in the garden, and Heavenly Blue's large, clear blue blooms are one of the most striking garden sights of midsummer to fall mornings. Morning glories are aptly named: each flower opens fresh and fully formed at dawn, displays for the morning, and closes by early afternoon — a flower with a daily lifecycle that rewards early-morning garden visits. The vines are vigorous and fast-growing (8–10 feet in a season) and self-seed freely, which means a single planting may produce volunteers in subsequent years. In the South, morning glories can become invasive in warm areas, so deadheading spent flowers before seed set is advisable. The best blooming occurs in late summer and fall when shorter day length triggers more abundant flowering.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Heavenly Blue morning glory variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Morning glory collection
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Vines for Florida and the Gulf South

common_name: Moonflower Vine (Night-Blooming White-Flowered Annual Ipomoea Vine)

planting.note: Direct sow or start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; nick or soak seeds overnight to improve germination. In zone 9, plant March–April; moonflower is a tropical perennial in frost-free areas but grown as a fast annual. Provide sturdy trellis, arbor, or fence — vines reach 10–15 feet in one season. Moonflower's fragrant white flowers open in the evening and close by midmorning, making it the ideal choice for planting near outdoor seating areas or porches used in the evening. Excellent paired with Heavenly Blue morning glory (which opens in the morning when moonflower closes).

directions: Nick seed coat or soak overnight; direct sow 0.5 inch deep after last frost along a sturdy trellis or arbor. Moonflower Vine is the large-flowered night-blooming cousin of morning glory — a tropical Ipomoea species producing enormous (4–6 inch diameter) white, saucer-shaped flowers with a sweet, tropical fragrance that unfurl rapidly at dusk and remain open through the night until midmorning, when they close as morning glories open. The dramatic daily opening — moonflowers can be watched as the buds spirally unfurl in minutes at dusk — is one of the most theatrical performances in the plant world. The very large flowers are pure white, satiny, with a star-shaped pattern visible in the petals, and the heavy, sweet, tropical fragrance carries well on still evening air, perfuming the garden and attracting sphinx moths (hummingbird moths) that are primary pollinators. Moonflower is a fast-growing, robust vine (10–15 feet per season) that covers a standard garden arbor or pergola quickly. The combination of its dramatic evening show and fragrance makes moonflower ideal for planting near outdoor dining or seating areas — evening patio dinners become special when the moonflowers open at dusk around the pergola. In zone 9–10, moonflower is a short-lived perennial that may persist between mild winters.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Moonflower vine variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Annual Vines for Florida Gardens
Missouri Botanical Garden — Ipomoea alba plant profile

common_name: Purple Hyacinth Bean Vine (Ornamental Heirloom Lablab Bean Vine)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; purple hyacinth bean is a fast-growing tropical vine that can cover an arbor, trellis, or fence rapidly in the Gulf South's warm growing season. In zone 9, plant March–May — the vine grows most vigorously and flowers most prolifically as day length decreases in August and September. The entire plant is ornamental: purple-tinged leaves, magenta-purple pea-like flowers, and spectacular bright purple seed pods that persist for weeks. A perennial in zones 9b–11 but easily grown as an annual from seed.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost near a sturdy trellis, fence, or arbor — the vines can reach 10–15 feet and require support. Purple Hyacinth Bean (Lablab purpureus) is one of the most dramatic ornamental vines for the warm-season garden — a fast-growing tropical legume vine with multiple ornamental features occurring simultaneously: dark green leaves with deep purple veining and purple undersides, dense racemes of fragrant, bright magenta-purple pea-type flowers, and after flowering, spectacular flat, bright purple seed pods (2–3 inches long) that persist on the vine for weeks, contrasting strikingly with the continuing flowers and creating a multi-season display. The ornamental pod color is unusual in the plant kingdom — very few plants produce purple pods of this intensity, and the color display is most impressive in late summer and fall when the pods are most abundant. Hyacinth bean is one of the most popular annual vines in American Southern gardens where the long growing season allows it to reach its full potential; it is relatively tender and needs the warmth of a Gulf South spring to establish well. The seeds and pods are edible when cooked thoroughly (raw hyacinth bean contains toxic amounts of cyanogenic glucosides), and the plant is grown as a food crop throughout Africa and Asia.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Ornamental Annual Vines for Florida
Missouri Botanical Garden — Lablab purpureus plant profile
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Purple hyacinth bean variety notes

common_name: Hopi Blue Corn (Traditional Native American Blue Flour Corn)

planting.note: Direct sow in blocks of at least 4 rows for wind pollination; isolate from other corn by 300 feet or by 2-week timing difference to prevent color dilution from cross-pollination. In zone 9, plant March–May for summer harvest; Hopi Blue matures as a dried corn (allow ears to dry on the plant before harvesting). The blue color in the flour and tortillas is enhanced by the alkaline lime-processing (nixtamalization) used in traditional corn preparation.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in blocks of 4+ rows; harvest ears when husks are fully dry and brown (typically 3–4 weeks after the milk stage of sweet corn). Hopi Blue is a traditional open-pollinated flour corn grown by the Hopi people of the Colorado Plateau for centuries — one of the most culturally significant food plants in the Southwestern United States, where blue corn (technically any of several traditional pueblo corn varieties) is the foundation of traditional Hopi cuisine and ceremony. The deep blue-purple to near-black kernels are soft starch corn (flour type) ground into the fine blue cornmeal used for piki bread (the traditional thin, paper-like blue corn bread), blue corn tortillas, atole (blue corn porridge), and blue corn mush. The blue color comes from anthocyanin pigments in the aleurone layer, and the striking blue color is enhanced rather than destroyed by traditional lime processing (nixtamalization). Modern American markets for blue corn tortilla chips and blue corn products have dramatically increased interest in blue corn varieties. Hopi Blue requires isolation from other corn types to maintain kernel color purity; crossing with yellow or white sweet corn produces offspring with diluted color. The variety shows good drought tolerance adapted to its ancestral dry-mesa homeland.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Hopi Blue corn variety documentation
Native Seeds/SEARCH — Hopi Blue traditional corn
University of Arizona Cooperative Extension — Traditional Pueblo Corn Varieties
